methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
RIOS Intelligent Machines, a robotics and manufacturing company founded by Bernard Casse, was acquired after raising $46M+ in total funding. The valuation assessment draws from a limited comparable universe: one public company (277810.KQ) and eight private robotics peers including 1X Technologies, Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, and Bedrock Robotics.

The peer-median method proved inapplicable due to insufficient narrow-stage comparables. While the Robotics & Manufacturing sector contains 11 same-sector peers, none satisfied the filtering criteria of matching stage, adjacent-stage placement, or similar valuation tier simultaneously. The single public comp and fragmented private dataset lacked the minimum three-point threshold required for reliable median anchoring. A sector-wide median would introduce material bias given the diversity of robotics subsectors and business models represented in the peer set.

Given the acquisition outcome and data constraints, traditional triangulation proved unfeasible. Any valuation range would rest on unvalidated peer selection rather than rigorous quantitative analysis. The absence of sufficient stage-aligned and tier-comparable private transactions in the robotics space reflects the sector's early-stage fragmentation and the difficulty in establishing normalized valuation multiples. Key uncertainties center on whether RIOS's acquisition price reflected the full value of its $46M+ invested capital and how its specific robotics vertical positioned it relative to the heterogeneous peer universe.
